name: Homeowners Association (HOA) Addendum
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Homeowners Association (HOA) Addendum to supplement residential real estate purchase agreements. Ensures full disclosure of mandatory HOA membership, governing documents, financial obligations, and restrictions to protect buyer and seller interests. Use in residential transactions involving HOA-governed properties to comply with state disclosure laws.
content:
# Homeowners Association (HOA) Addendum

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Homeowners Association (HOA) Addendum to supplement a residential real estate purchase agreement. This transactional document serves as a critical disclosure and acknowledgment instrument that ensures all parties understand the mandatory HOA obligations, restrictions, and financial commitments associated with the subject property.

## Document Purpose and Legal Context

This addendum functions as a binding modification to the underlying purchase agreement and must comply with applicable state real estate disclosure laws, including but not limited to requirements established by state real estate commissions and consumer protection statutes. The document should be drafted with precision to protect both the seller's disclosure obligations and the buyer's right to make an informed purchasing decision. Ensure the addendum references and incorporates the main purchase agreement by date and parties, creating a clear contractual linkage between the two documents.

## Parties and Property Identification

Begin by clearly identifying all parties to the transaction using their full legal names as they appear in the underlying purchase agreement. Designate the buyer(s) and seller(s) with specificity, including marital status where relevant to title considerations. Provide the complete legal description of the property, including street address, city, county, state, and zip code. If available, include the assessor's parcel number (APN) and subdivision name. Reference the original purchase agreement by its execution date to establish the relationship between the documents. The identification section should leave no ambiguity as to which transaction and which property this addendum governs.

## HOA Disclosure and Organizational Information

Provide comprehensive disclosure of the mandatory HOA membership requirement, emphasizing that membership is not optional and automatically transfers with property ownership. Include the complete legal name of the homeowners association, its principal business address, and current contact information including phone number, email, and website if available. Disclose the name and contact information of the current HOA management company, if applicable. State whether the HOA is incorporated and under which state's laws it operates. Include information about the association's fiscal year and the current status of the HOA's financial health, if known. This section must clearly communicate that the buyer will be bound by all HOA governing documents, rules, and regulations upon closing, and that failure to comply may result in fines, liens, or other enforcement actions.

## Financial Obligations and Assessment Structure

Detail all current and anticipated financial obligations associated with HOA membership with specificity and transparency. State the current regular assessment amount, payment frequency (monthly, quarterly, annually), and due dates. Disclose any pending or approved special assessments, including the amount, purpose, payment schedule, and whether the seller or buyer will be responsible for payment. Include information about the HOA's reserve fund status and any known upcoming capital improvements that may trigger future special assessments. Specify the consequences of late or non-payment, including late fees, interest rates, and the HOA's lien rights. Address transfer fees, capital contribution fees, or any other one-time charges the buyer will incur at closing or upon membership. Clarify whether assessments are subject to increase and, if so, the process and limitations on such increases as set forth in the governing documents.

## Governing Documents and Restrictive Covenants

Identify all governing documents that will bind the buyer, including but not limited to the Declaration of Covenants, Conditions, and Restrictions (CC&Rs), Articles of Incorporation, Bylaws, Rules and Regulations, Architectural Guidelines, and any amendments thereto. Specify the recording information for recorded documents, including book and page numbers or instrument numbers where available. Describe the general nature of restrictions that may affect the buyer's use and enjoyment of the property, such as:

- Architectural control requirements for exterior modifications, landscaping, paint colors, and structural changes
- Use restrictions including rental prohibitions or limitations, home business restrictions, and vehicle parking rules
- Pet policies including species, size, and number limitations
- Common area usage rules and exclusive use assignments

Affirmatively state that the buyer has the right to review all governing documents prior to closing and should include a contingency period for such review. Provide information about how the buyer can obtain complete copies of all governing documents and specify any associated costs for document production.

## Buyer Acknowledgment, Representations, and Contingencies

Draft clear acknowledgment language wherein the buyer confirms receipt of this addendum and all referenced HOA documents, or alternatively, acknowledges the right to receive and review such documents within a specified timeframe. Include the buyer's express agreement to be bound by all current and future HOA rules, regulations, and amendments adopted in accordance with the governing documents. State that the buyer has had the opportunity to ask questions about the HOA and its requirements, and has been advised to review all documents carefully and consult with legal counsel if desired.

If applicable, incorporate a contingency provision allowing the buyer to terminate the purchase agreement if, after reviewing the HOA documents, the buyer finds the terms unacceptable. Specify the contingency period duration (typically 3-10 days from receipt of documents) and the procedure for exercising the termination right, including notice requirements and earnest money disposition. Include a provision addressing the buyer's responsibility to verify all HOA information independently and the seller's limitation of liability for information provided in good faith but later found to be inaccurate.

## Execution and Effectiveness

Conclude the addendum with a properly formatted signature block for all parties to the underlying purchase agreement. Each signature line should include space for the party's printed name, signature, and date of execution. If the transaction involves multiple buyers or sellers, ensure each party has a separate signature line. Include acknowledgment that this addendum becomes effective only when executed by all parties and that it survives closing and merges with the deed unless otherwise specified. Add a provision confirming that in the event of any conflict between this addendum and the underlying purchase agreement, the terms of this addendum shall control with respect to HOA-related matters.

## Formatting and Professional Standards

Structure the document with clear headings, numbered or lettered sections for easy reference, and sufficient white space for readability. Use plain language where possible while maintaining legal precision. Ensure all cross-references to the purchase agreement and governing documents are accurate and specific. The final document should be professionally formatted, free of errors, and ready for execution by all parties. Include a footer with page numbers and a header identifying the document as an "HOA Addendum" with the property address for easy identification.
